metadata.summary: Structured approach to building high-performing teams using Tuckman's five-stage model with team charter, skills matrix, and retrospective cycles for continuous improvement.

content.title: Team Development
content.summary: A structured approach to building high-performing teams using Tuckman's five-stage model (Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning), a team charter with working agreements, a skills matrix for gap planning, and retrospective cycles for continuous improvement. The PM's role shifts by stage: directive at Forming, mediating at Storming, delegating at Performing, celebrating at Adjourning.

why.p: Talented individuals do not automatically form effective teams. Unaddressed Storming causes permanent dysfunction. Skills gaps appear mid-project without a matrix to surface them. The methodology makes team health visible and gives the PM concrete actions at each stage rather than generic "improve communication" advice.

list.item: Forming a new team: producing charter, working agreements, RACI, and skills matrix in one pass
Diagnosing a struggling team via Tuckman stage assessment with recommended PM actions
Building a skills-gap plan (training, pairing, or hire decisions)
Generating and synthesising retrospective patterns across multiple sprints
HR/performance-management actions (compensation, PIPs, hiring decisions) — require a human + HR system
Crisis interventions (harassment, mental health, layoffs) — agents must escalate to humans immediately
Cross-cultural mediation where confidentiality and nuance dominate — automation backfires
Single-person projects — no team to develop

section.name: tuckman-stages
section.p: Five-stage model with characteristics, PM actions, and transition signals for each stage.

section.name: forming
section.p: Team is polite, cautious, and dependent on the PM for direction. Roles unclear, goals abstract, conflict avoided.

list.item: PM actions: clarify roles and responsibilities, set expectations, facilitate introductions, produce team charter
Transition signal: members begin asking each other questions rather than always going to the PM

section.name: storming
section.p: Conflict, competition, and frustration surface as individuals push back on roles and process. This is healthy and expected; suppressing it delays Norming.

list.item: PM actions: facilitate structured discussion with ground rules, mediate with focus on shared goals, document agreed decisions
Transition signal: conflict becomes task-focused rather than interpersonal; throughput stabilises

section.name: norming
section.p: Trust builds, cooperation replaces competition, norms are established. Team starts self-organising.

list.item: PM actions: delegate more, reinforce positive norms, support team decisions, encourage cross-training
Transition signal: team resolves minor issues independently without escalating to PM

section.name: performing
section.p: High productivity, autonomy, distributed leadership. PM shifts to removing obstacles and shielding from external disruption.

list.item: PM actions: empower and trust, remove blockers, protect team from scope thrash, focus on outcomes
Transition signal: velocity or throughput is stable and predictable over multiple sprints

section.name: adjourning
section.p: Project ends or team dissolves. Emotional closure, knowledge preservation, and individual transition support.

list.item: PM actions: celebrate achievements, conduct final retrospective, document lessons learned, facilitate career transitions
Transition signal: project closure milestone reached; team members reassigned

rule.statement: When staging a team from indirect signals, emit a confidence score (0.0-1.0) and list the evidence; do not state the stage as fact without observable data.
rule.rationale: Tuckman is a model, not a measurement instrument; overconfident staging from sparse signals leads to wrong PM actions.

rule.statement: Co-write the team charter with the team in a 60-minute workshop; never let an agent author it solo without team input.
rule.rationale: Agent-authored charters are generic and lack the buy-in that only participation creates.

rule.statement: Every retrospective action item must have a single named owner and a sprint-deadline; reject "the team will look into this".
rule.rationale: Actions without owners are not actions; they are intentions that expire at the end of the retro.

rule.statement: Refresh skills matrix quarterly; treat it as planning input, not performance data.
rule.rationale: Skills matrices used for performance review create gaming; used for planning they create learning opportunities.

rule.statement: Retro outputs must not name individuals; preserve psychological safety in all summarised outputs.
rule.rationale: Individual naming in retro summaries chills honest participation and can cause interpersonal harm.

rule.statement: Retro themes must be backed by evidence from at least two distinct sprints before being called a pattern.
rule.rationale: Single-sprint observations are noise; patterns require recurrence to be actionable.

rule.statement: Evaluate training and pairing options before proposing a hire to close a skills gap.
rule.rationale: Agents default to "hire" because it is the clearest action; training and pairing are faster, cheaper, and improve retention.

antipattern.description: Recommending "more standups" or "better communication" as the fix for most retro themes.
antipattern.reason: This is an LLM default; it rarely addresses the root cause and wastes ceremony time on the wrong intervention.

antipattern.description: Setting skills matrix scores to 3 (median) when the agent has no information about a person.
antipattern.reason: Unknown should be "unknown", not a guess; phantom scores drive incorrect gap-planning decisions.

antipattern.description: Flagging recurring themes as "new" each sprint because the agent has no session memory.
antipattern.reason: Pass last N synthesised theme files into context; without history the agent cannot detect patterns.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: The agent is a documentation and pattern-extraction layer. Phase 1: a team-charter-builder produces the charter from team list + project context. Phase 2: a retro-synthesiser extracts themes and action items from raw retro notes (max-N sprints) and proposes experiments. Phase 3: a tuckman-coach reads recent signals (retro themes, throughput, conflict reports) and stages the team with PM actions. All outputs require human review; never publish to the team without sign-off.

rule.statement: Use recommended subagents: team-charter-builder (define inline) — input: team list, project context; output: charter markdown. retro-synthesiser (define inline) — input: N retro markdown files; output: themes + recurring action items + experiments. tuckman-coach (define inline) — input: signals; output: stage + recommended PM actions. skills-matrix-analyzer (define inline) — input: role expectations + matrix; output: gap plan (training / pair / hire). faion-brainstorm — to generate options for stuck retros (e.g. "we keep talking about scope").
rule.rationale: Specialized subagents enforce safety constraints (no individual naming, confidence scores, minimum evidence) and prevent common LLM failure modes.

example.note: Prompt pattern for retro synthesis — emits themes backed by evidence, not guesses
example.code: You are a retro synthesiser. Inputs: <list of retro_*.md>. Output JSON: { "themes": [ { "theme": "...", "evidence_count": N, "first_seen_sprint": "...", "still_present": bool, "proposed_experiment": "imperative, time-boxed", "owner_suggestion": "role" } ], "tuckman_signal": "Forming|Storming|Norming|Performing|Adjourning", "tuckman_evidence": ["..."] } Rules: - Do NOT name individuals; preserve psychological safety. - Themes must be backed by ≥ 2 distinct sprints. - Experiments must be falsifiable within 2 sprints.

rule.statement: Co-write the charter with the team in a 60-min workshop; do not let an agent author it solo.
rule.rationale: Agent-authored charters are generic and lack buy-in.

rule.statement: Refresh skills matrix quarterly; treat it as planning input, not performance data.
rule.rationale: Skills matrices used for performance create gaming; used for planning they create learning.

rule.statement: Retros: rotate facilitator, time-box, capture an action with owner + sprint deadline.
rule.rationale: Action item without an owner = no action; reject.

rule.statement: Storming is healthy and short-lived; do not "fix" it by suppressing conflict — facilitate it.
Solo → team: document everything assumed-obvious; first hire onboarding fails on tribal knowledge.
rule.rationale: Scaling from solo requires explicit documentation of all norms, processes, and expectations.

rule.statement: Track team health with one or two metrics (eNPS, throughput stability), not a dashboard with twenty.
rule.rationale: More metrics increase noise; one clear signal drives better decisions.

section.name: agent-safety-constraints
section.p: LLMs name individuals in retro summaries by default — strip names; preserve psychological safety. "More standups / better communication" is a recommended-fix bias; require alternative options. Tuckman staging from sparse signals is overconfident; agents should emit a confidence score and surface evidence. Action items get reformulated into corporate-ese ("enhance synergy"); enforce imperative + measurable form. Recurring themes are flagged as "new" each sprint because the agent has no memory; pass last N synthesised theme files into context. Sensitive content (mental-health, harassment) leaks through summarisation; add a content-filter step that escalates to a human channel and aborts. Agents propose hires when the gap is process / pairing — require considering training + pairing before hire. Skills-matrix scores drift toward 3 (median) when the agent has no info; force "unknown" instead of guessing.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Tools for team collaboration and tracking: gh (pull throughput / cycle-time signals from PRs, https://cli.github.com/); jira-cli (pull velocity / WIP signals, https://github.com/ankitpokhrel/jira-cli); pandoc (render charter / matrix to DOCX / PDF, https://pandoc.org/); mermaid-cli (Tuckman state diagrams + skills heatmaps, https://github.com/mermaid-js/mermaid-cli); wordcloud-cli Python (theme visualisation across retros, https://github.com/amueller/word_cloud); ranger-mover custom (move retro notes between sprint folders).

Services: Lattice SaaS (1-on-1s, growth, reviews; agent can summarise, https://lattice.com); 15Five SaaS (Pulse + 1-on-1 notes, https://www.15five.com); Culture Amp SaaS (engagement surveys for team-stage signal); Officevibe SaaS (lightweight pulse surveys); Range SaaS (async standups + check-ins); Confluence / Notion SaaS (where charter, matrix, retro outputs live); FunRetro / Parabol / Retrium SaaS (retro boards with export to JSON); Slack + Polly / Geekbot SaaS (async retros and pulse).

section.name: limitations-and-gotchas
section.p: Tuckman is a model, not a measurement instrument; agents stage teams confidently from sparse signals and can be wrong. Skills matrices are a proxy: real performance depends on context, not numbers; an agent that hires from "matrix gaps" can miss culture fit. Retro patterns repeat (communication, planning, scope) regardless of project; agents that surface only the top theme miss outliers. Confidentiality: 1-on-1 notes contain sensitive content. Agents must not auto-aggregate or store them across sessions. Bias: LLMs trained on tech-bro retros recommend "more standups" as a fix to almost everything. Conflict mediation requires presence; agents can summarise positions but cannot actually mediate. Working agreements drafted by an agent are generic unless seeded with team-specific examples.

template.purpose: Charter with mission, members, working agreements, meeting cadence, success metrics
Role x skill grid (1-4 scale) with gap action column
Went well / didn't go well / ideas / action items with owner and sprint deadline
